title: Paul M. Sniderman and Louk Hagendoorn. When Ways of Life Collide: Multiculturalism and its Discontents in the Netherlands
abstract: The central argument put forward by Paul Sniderman and Louk Hagendoorn in When Ways of Life Collide is that the policy of multiculturalism in the Netherlands, imposed through consensus by the political elite, has led group identities to become a focal point of political argument, increasing hostilities between immigrant minorities and the Dutch majority. These hostilities were brought to the surface by the events surrounding September 11 2001, the rise of the populist politician Pim Fortuyn on an anti-immigrant platform in 2002, and the murder of filmmaker Theo van Gogh in 2004 by a Dutch Moroccan offended by van Gogh’s controversial film. Ironically therefore, Sniderman and Hagendoorn argue, the policy aimed at promoting respect for the pluralism of cultures has had the exact opposite effect. This thought-provoking book provides many interesting insights into the relationships between a culture’s values, prejudice, perceived cultural and economic threats, and exclusionary reactions against immigrants, derived from the analysis of a skillfully designed survey. It is relevant to a wide audience concerned with attitudes towards immigrant minorities, immigration, and multiculturalism, as well as to those interested in innovations in survey design.
